label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's consider this question about causation and intent. The scenario involves Brown's intention to kill Smith by detonating a bomb. The bomb is in the other building where Smith is located. Brown can detonate the bomb only by producing a six-dotted image on the camera lens, which is focused on the top of a table in his room. To do this, he rolls a six-sided die in the camera's view. If the die lands with the six facing up, the camera captures a six-dotted image, which triggers the bomb to detonate. Now, the question is whether Brown intentionally rolled a six. In this situation, Brown wants to kill Smith now. He is aware of the setup: rolling a six will detonate the bomb and kill Smith. He is hoping the die will land six-up. He is taking the action of rolling the die in the camera's view for the purpose of getting a six, which would kill Smith. So, he is trying to produce a six through this action. This is a question about criminal intent, specifically whether Brown "intentionally" rolled a six. In criminal law, intent can mean either "purpose" or "knowledge" depending on the jurisdiction, but the key is whether the actor had the purpose of bringing about the result (here, rolling a six). Brown is aiming for a six because he knows that a six will trigger the bomb. He is acting with the specific purpose of achieving a six. Therefore, from a typical person's perspective, it would be reasonable to say Brown intentionally rolled a six, even though there was an element of chance involved (1 in 6 chance of rolling a six).
